---
title: "Installing OpenShift on AWS: a repeatable script & walkthrough"
description: |
  A compact, annotated shell workflow to install OpenShift 4.x on AWS — with a
  ready-to-edit install-config.yaml, sensible defaults, and the trade-offs you
  should know before you press enter.
author:
  - name: Joseph Assiga
    url: https://josephassiga.github.io/about.html
    affiliation: Independent
date: 2026-06-10
date-modified: 2026-06-10
categories: [openshift, cloud, aws, devops, kubernetes]
image: /images/ocp-install.svg
image-alt: "OpenShift installation flow diagram"
keywords: [OpenShift, AWS, IPI, install-config, kubernetes, devops]
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-line-numbers: true
---

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**TL;DR** — Save the shell script below, edit `OCP_VERSION` and your
`install-config.yaml`, then run `openshift-install create cluster --dir ocp-install`.
The full annotated walkthrough is below.
:::

This post documents a compact, repeatable shell-based workflow used to install
OpenShift 4.x on AWS. I extracted the working script, annotated each step,
and included a sample `install-config.yaml` for a minimal IPI installation.

## Why this matters

> Repeatable installs are the difference between a cluster you understand
> and one you merely possess.

- **Repeatability** — encapsulates download, install, and bootstrap steps.
- **Auditability** — the full script appears below so you can review before running.
- **Tunable** — variables like `OCP_VERSION` and `ARCH` are exposed for quick edits.

![High-level installation flow.](/images/ocp-install.svg){#fig-ocp-flow fig-align="center" width="80%"}

## AWS prerequisites — at a glance

Before you run the installer, you need an EC2 host to drive the install and a
public Route 53 hosted zone matching your `baseDomain`.

::: {layout-ncol=2}
![Create the installer EC2 host.](/images/create-ec2.png){#fig-ec2}

![Create the public Route 53 hosted zone.](/images/create-public-hostedzone.png){#fig-hostedzone}
:::

Both screenshots show the minimum configuration that satisfies the OpenShift
installer's IAM and DNS expectations.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Pick a specific version

Avoid `latest-4.22` for production runs — pin to a release like `4.22.3` so the
install is reproducible months later when `latest` has moved on.
:::

## Prerequisites

- A Linux host with `curl`, `tar`, and `ssh` available.
- IAM permissions matching the
  [OpenShift required AWS permissions](https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/latest/installing/installing_aws/installing-aws-account.html).
- A valid OpenShift pull secret and an SSH key.

## How the install script works

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  A[Start] --> B[Create tools dir]
  B --> C[Set env vars]
  C --> D[Download oc client]
  D --> E[Install oc & kubectl]
  E --> F[Download openshift-install]
  F --> G[Install openshift-install binary]
  G --> H[Generate SSH key & add to agent]
  H --> I[Write install-config.yaml]
  I --> J[Run openshift-install create cluster]
  J --> K[Wait for cluster readiness]
  K --> L[Done]
```

## The script (annotated)

Read it carefully and adapt the variables to your environment before running.

```{.bash filename="ocp-install.sh"}
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

yum update -y

mkdir -p tools && cd tools/

# Pin a specific minor for reproducibility.
export OCP_VERSION="latest-4.22"
export ARCH="x86_64"
export HOST_ARCH="$(uname -m)"

# --- oc / kubectl ---
curl -fsSLk \
  "https://mirror.openshift.com/pub/openshift-v4/${HOST_ARCH}/clients/ocp/${OCP_VERSION}/openshift-client-linux.tar.gz" \
  -o oc.tar.gz
tar zxf oc.tar.gz
rm -f oc.tar.gz README.md
chmod +x oc kubectl
sudo mv oc kubectl /usr/local/bin/

# --- openshift-install ---
curl -fsSLk \
  "https://mirror.openshift.com/pub/openshift-v4/${HOST_ARCH}/clients/ocp/${OCP_VERSION}/openshift-install-linux.tar.gz" \
  -o openshift-install-linux.tar.gz
tar zxf openshift-install-linux.tar.gz
rm -f openshift-install-linux.tar.gz README.md
chmod +x openshift-install
sudo mv openshift-install /usr/local/bin/

# --- SSH key for cluster nodes ---
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -N '' -f ~/.ssh/rhoai-demo
eval "$(ssh-agent -s)"
ssh-add ~/.ssh/rhoai-demo

# --- Launch the install ---
mkdir -p ocp-install
# put install-config.yaml in ./ocp-install first, then:
openshift-install create cluster --dir ocp-install --log-level=info
```

## Example `install-config.yaml`

Adapt `baseDomain`, instance types, and `pullSecret` before use.

```{.yaml filename="ocp-install/install-config.yaml"}
apiVersion: v1
baseDomain: rhoai.rh-aiservices.com
compute:
  - architecture: amd64
    hyperthreading: Enabled
    name: worker
    platform:
      aws:
        type: m6i.4xlarge
    replicas: 0
controlPlane:
  architecture: amd64
  hyperthreading: Enabled
  name: master
  platform:
    aws:
      type: g6.12xlarge
      rootVolume:
        size: 1000
  replicas: 1
metadata:
  name: rhoai-demo
networking:
  clusterNetwork:
    - cidr: 10.128.0.0/14
      hostPrefix: 23
  machineNetwork:
    - cidr: 10.0.0.0/16
  networkType: OVNKubernetes
  serviceNetwork:
    - 172.30.0.0/16
platform:
  aws:
    region: eu-west-3
publish: External
pullSecret: '{"auths":{"cloud.openshift.com":{"auth":"<token>","email":"<email>"}}}'
sshKey: 'ssh-ed25519 AAAA...redacted...'
```

::: {.callout-warning}
## Never commit secrets

`pullSecret` and the contents of `~/.ssh/rhoai-demo` are credentials.
Add `install-config.yaml`, `*.pem`, and `auth/` to your `.gitignore` *before*
running the installer — the installer mutates this file and writes
`auth/kubeadmin-password` and `auth/kubeconfig` next to it.
:::

## Important notes & best practices

- Replace `OCP_VERSION` with a specific release for reproducible runs.
- Always pass `--dir` so re-running and `gather` commands work consistently.
- Run downloads from a bastion with stable network connectivity.
- The single-master `controlPlane.replicas: 1` above is a **lab** topology —
  production needs three.

::: {.callout-important}
## Single-node OpenShift ≠ Production HA

A `controlPlane.replicas: 1` install is fine for a demo cluster but should
never carry workloads you can't lose. For HA, use three control-plane nodes
and at least two workers across availability zones.
:::

## Troubleshooting

| Symptom | First thing to check |
|---|---|
| Installer hangs on bootstrap | `openshift-install gather bootstrap --dir ocp-install` |
| `mv` fails moving binaries | Re-run the `mv` lines with `sudo` |
| AWS API quota errors | Verify the EC2/EBS quotas in your target region |
| DNS resolution fails post-install | Verify Route 53 NS records match the hosted zone |

: Common failure modes and where to look. {#tbl-troubleshooting}

## Wrap-up

If you treat the script in this post as a *starting point* rather than a
black box — pin versions, audit IAM, and review the `install-config.yaml`
before each run — you'll get a reproducible OpenShift install you actually
understand.

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
Found a sharp edge? Open an issue at
[`josephassiga/josephassiga.github.io`](https://github.com/josephassiga/josephassiga.github.io)
— I keep this post updated as the installer evolves.
:::
